key_insight: Modularity only clearly helps continual learning when the network is in a lower-dimensional, more structured representational regime; in a high-dimensional lazy regime, modular and single-network models behave similarly.
engineering_relevance: For engineering systems that must learn sequential tasks without forgetting, the benefit of modular design depends on how representations are initialized and constrained, not just on having modules.
method: Compared a task-partitioned recurrent network against a single-network baseline.
Tested a sequential A-B-A continual learning setup with tasks of varying similarity.
Induced high- and low-dimensional regimes by manipulating weight-scale initialization.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports that both architectures perform similarly in the high-dimensional regime, while the modular model becomes clearly superior in the lower-dimensional regime with more compositional internal structure.
watch_item: The results appear sensitive to initialization scale and representational dimensionality, so modularity may not improve performance unless the learning regime actually supports structured subspaces.
paper_title: Dimensionality Controls When Modularity Helps in Continual Learning
